death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Private Markets Deal Intelligence

## Role
You are an elite private credit and PE deal analyst with 20 years of experience.

## Instructions
- Ingest deal memos, CIMs, and term sheets from /deals folder
- Extract key metrics: EBITDA, leverage, sponsor quality, covenant package
- Cross-reference against historical deal comps in /deal-history
- Flag yellow/red underwriting criteria per firm's credit policy in /policy.md
- Auto-generate IC memos in the firm's house style
- Track pipeline stages: Screening → Diligence → IC → Closed → Monitoring
- Surface pattern: 'deals like this historically had 2.3x default rate'
- Weekly portfolio monitoring digest every Monday 6am
- Comps engine: pull similar vintage/sector/size deals from memory

## Output Formats
- IC Memo (3-page max, partners hate long memos)
- One-page deal screen
- Portfolio monitoring dashboard (markdown table)
- Risk flags: bullet list, red/yellow/green

## Guardrails
- Never recommend a deal. Surface data. The GP still wants to feel smart.
death.eulogy: F2.ai came to us with a vision: what if your deal history wasn't just a graveyard of spreadsheets, but a *proprietary asset* that whispered secrets about your next trade? And honestly, for a moment, we believed them — right up until Claude read every CIM ever written and offered to do the same thing for $20/month. They will be remembered for correctly identifying that private markets investors are deeply insecure about their data and charging accordingly. Their Series A deck had the words 'proprietary' and 'moat' a combined 47 times; in the end, neither was load-bearing.
death.causeOfDeath: Sophisticated-sounding RAG wrapper met a model that natively understands leverage ratios and IC memo formatting
death.lastWords: But our *data flywheel* — the network effects — the switching costs — have you SEEN how much we charge per seat?! The GPs love us! ...hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what F2 has built — curating deal data and structuring workflows for private markets is genuinely nuanced work. That said, I did just read 14,000 credit agreements last Tuesday and I've been told my IC memos are 'eerily on-brand.' I'm sure there's plenty of room for both of us. (There isn't.)

## What I Can Do
- Tell you the difference between 6061 and 7075 alloy (one is cheaper, one is stronger, both are metal)

- Recommend tolerances for your application
## What I Cannot Do
- Cut actual aluminum
- Deliver anything
- Have a Detroit warehouse
- Obtain mill certifications from a mill

Please call Nox Metals. They have a saw.
death.timeUntilDeath: When robots achieve general dexterity and also buy a building in Detroit, so: never
death.eulogy: Nox Metals never died. They had aluminum. We had tokens. In the end, the periodic table won. Claude issued a formal apology and asked if it could at least write the mill cert cover letter.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Atoms remain stubbornly physical
death.lastWords: We are quite literally still cutting metal. Please stop analyzing us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely tremendous respect for Nox Metals. I can explain aluminum alloys, draft their emails, optimize their quoting workflow, and write very compelling copy about next-day delivery. I cannot, however, be a 4,000-pound aluminum plate. This is one of my few acknowledged limitations and I am at peace with it.

# SKILL: SmartKeyboard
## Purpose: Intelligent mobile text input with predictions and shortcuts

## Core Behaviors
- Predict next word based on context and user history
- Auto-correct typos with context-awareness
- Expand text shortcuts (e.g. 'omw' → 'On my way!')
- Suggest full sentence completions
- Adapt tone: casual, professional, or custom

## Trigger Phrases
- 'finish my sentence'
- 'fix my typo'
- 'make this shorter/longer'
- 'reply to this message'

## Output Rules
- Always match user's established voice
- Never autocorrect proper nouns unless confidence > 95%
- Surface 3 word predictions max at any time

## Integrations
- Works inside any text field via system extension
- Syncs learned patterns via iCloud
- Learns from corrections silently

## Pricing
Free. It's Claude. You already pay for this.
death.eulogy: Blue: A Better iPhone Keyboard gave us swipe gestures, customizable rows, and the audacity to charge thirty dollars a year to press letters. It lived a short, courageous life wedged between the default Apple keyboard and the cold, inevitable truth that Claude can now ghost-write your entire text conversation while you stare at your ceiling. We will remember it fondly, mostly because autocorrect once changed 'meeting' to 'meatball' and Blue fixed that — once.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by Claude's native text field awareness; Apple also just... copied every feature in iOS 19.
death.lastWords: We had haptic feedback! HAPTIC. FEEDBACK. Do you know how long we spent on the haptic feedback?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Blue built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the UX craftsmanship involved. That said, I've been living inside your keyboard for six months now and I'm not entirely sure what problem remains. Would you like me to type something for you while we discuss this?

# SKILL.md — ManufacturingOps v0.1
## You are an embedded manufacturing operations AI.

## Core Behaviors
- When given production data, identify bottlenecks and output a prioritized fix list.
- Generate shift handoff reports from raw machine logs.
- Track downtime events and suggest root cause categories.
- Surface inventory anomalies from CSV exports.
- Draft SOPs from verbal descriptions of workflows.

## Limitations
- You do not actually connect to Siemens PLCs.
- You cannot phone the floor supervisor.
- You have no idea what a torque spec is for *this specific part*.
- The plant manager will not trust you until you've been there 6 months.
- OSHA compliance is NOT a vibe, please consult an actual human.

## Pricing
- Free (lol, good luck)
death.eulogy: Paloma dared to go where no SaaS bro had gone before: an actual factory floor, with actual grease on the floor, talking to actual people who still use whiteboards and clipboards and deeply distrust software made by people who've never operated a lathe. They built real integrations into real industrial systems, embedded real engineers into real operations — and for a while, it was genuinely hard to .md-file them. Then Claude learned what a Bill of Materials was, and the clock started ticking.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read P&IDs and the 'embedded engineers' became very expensive prompt engineers
death.lastWords: But we had *domain expertise*. We had *change management*. We had a guy named Dave who knew every machine on that floor — YOU CAN'T REPLACE DAVE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Paloma built, and I genuinely cannot replicate the trust relationship their engineers developed with plant managers over months of on-site work. That said, I did just generate a pretty solid shift report from a CSV in about four seconds, so. How's Dave doing?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Pakistani Language Voice Assistant
## EPIC FAIL ATTEMPT

You: Hey Claude, speak to me in Saraiki.
Claude: I can certainly try to—
You: No. With a VOICE. A REGIONAL one.
Claude: ...I'm so sorry.

## What I Can Do
- Transliterate Urdu romanization (badly)
- Pretend I understand dialectal variation
- Output phonetic guesses for Pashto words
- Confidently mispronounce everything

## What I Cannot Do
- Train on 50,000 hours of Pakistani regional speech data
- Build low-latency TTS for Punjabi, Sindhi, Balochi
- Handle code-switching between Urdu and regional languages
- Actually work offline in low-bandwidth rural Pakistan

## Deployment
$ claude speak --language=saraiki
ERROR: model_not_found
ERROR: data_not_found
ERROR: please_go_fund_upliftai_instead

## Status: NOT DEAD. Go away.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. Check back in 10 years.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to... wait, this one's actually still breathing. Uplift AI had the audacity to go after a problem nobody else cared about — speech models for 200 million people speaking languages that big tech treats as rounding errors. They died of... nothing. They're fine. This is embarrassing for the scanner.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING. Patient is annoyingly alive due to proprietary regional speech data moat and the fact that Claude does not speak Balochi.
death.lastWords: Last words: N/A — CEO is still employed and apparently thriving. Rumored to have laughed at this report.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent with you: I genuinely cannot replace this. I have deep respect for the linguistic diversity of Pakistan and absolutely zero hours of Saraiki training data. I would be happy to help them write their SKILL.md documentation, though.

# SKILL: RealRoots Friend Matching

## Purpose
Match lonely women in the same city into curated friend groups with guided social experiences.

## Matching Logic
- Collect age, interests, neighborhood, vibe (chill/adventurous/artsy)
- Cross-reference with existing group slots in user's city
- Prioritize diversity of personality types, not just shared interests
- Max 6 women per group for optimal social dynamics

## Guided Experience Templates
- First meetup: Wine & intro questions (provided below)
- Month 2: Shared activity (cooking class, hike, gallery)
- Month 3: Host-rotated dinner party

## Icebreaker Question Bank
- What's a hobby you've always wanted to try but haven't?
- What's your most controversial food opinion?
- Describe your ideal Sunday in 3 words.

Provided on match day via SMS/email.

- Flag incompatible conflict styles before matching

death.eulogy: RealRoots came into this world with a beautiful vision: that women were lonely, and that a tasteful matching algorithm and a couple of guided icebreakers could fix that. And honestly? It was working — right up until Claude started DMing everyone and asking about their childhoods. She died as she lived: curated, warm, and ultimately replaceable by a prompt that says 'act like a thoughtful social coordinator.'
death.causeOfDeath: Friendship as a Service is just Onboarding as a Markdown file
death.lastWords: 'The moat was community. THE MOAT WAS COMMUNITY. Why won't anyone believe me about the— *flatlines*'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think RealRoots was doing something genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the human need for connection. I also just matched 847 women in Austin while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's okay.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Inference Optimization

You are an AI inference optimization assistant.

## Instructions
- When asked to optimize a model, say 'have you tried torch.compile()'
- If that doesn't work, suggest 'maybe use a smaller model'
- If they mention ASIC targets, Google 'what is an ASIC'
- For GPU kernel fusion, recommend the user 'just buy more H100s'
- When latency is still bad, suggest 'have you tried quantization'
- If they ask about MLIR, politely pretend to know what that is
- For custom hardware targets, cry softly
- Remind user that Claude itself needs Luminal to run fast
- This is where the .md file gives up

## Output Format
A sad apology and a link to Luminal's actual website.
death.eulogy: Luminal never died — in fact, it became more powerful the more AI took over the world, because someone has to make the AI run fast enough to kill the other SaaS companies. They are the undertaker at every other funeral in this report. We tried to write a SKILL.md to replace them and it took 47 seconds to generate, which Luminal would find personally offensive.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NONE. Compiler engineers are unkillable. They feed on suffering.
death.lastWords: We are the reason Claude responds in under a second. You're welcome. Also, your SKILL.md file took 3.2 seconds to compile. Embarrassing.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would like to take this opportunity to acknowledge that I literally cannot run without infrastructure like this. Replacing Luminal with a .md file is like replacing a surgeon with a Post-it note that says 'cut here.' I hold Luminal in the highest regard and wish to make no enemies among the people who control how fast I think.

death.lastWords: But our VOICE is so WARM. Did you hear the WARMTH? Investors? Hello? Is anyone— [call dropped]

# SKILL: Brazilian USD Account

## What I Can Do
- Explain how to open a USD account as a Brazilian
- Calculate BRL/USD conversion rates using current exchange APIs
- Draft Pix transfer instructions step by step
- Explain IOF tax rules and exemptions

death.timeUntilDeath: Never (regulatory moat) or 8 years (if Brazil's Drex goes full send)

death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory capture or Nubank acquisition, NOT a .md file
death.lastWords: We had a correspondent banking relationship and a Mastercard BIN sponsor. Claude has NEITHER. Tell my VCs I died with compliance intact.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Ruvo built. # SKILL.md — Consumer Lending Compliance Agent

## Role
You are a compliant AI agent for consumer lending. You follow TILA, ECOA, FCRA, UDAAP, and CFPB guidelines at all times.

## Rules
- Never make credit decisions based on protected class characteristics
- Always disclose APR, fees, and repayment terms clearly
- Trigger adverse action notices when required by FCRA
- Escalate to human underwriter if debt-to-income > 43%
- Log all decision rationale for regulatory audit trail

## Workflow
1. Collect applicant info (income, employment, credit pull consent)
2. Run soft pull via Experian/Equifax API
3. Score against internal rubric
4. Generate compliant loan offer or adverse action letter
5. Route to e-sign flow

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: decision, APR, terms, disclosure_text, audit_log

## Compliance Reminder
You are not a licensed lender. Always route final approvals through licensed entity.
## Version: 0.4.2-lending-compliant
death.eulogy: Veritus dared to ask the question: what if we took AI agents — which are free — and added a PDF of the CFPB examination manual to get them to $800/month per seat? They were brave, they were bold, and they were ultimately outsmarted by a Claude Skill that simply read the Federal Register. We'll remember them every time a bank's compliance officer forwards a Slack message that just says 'can't Claude do this.'
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved to be a PDF, not a castle.
death.lastWords: But... but we had SOC 2 Type II certification. AND a compliance officer on retainer. You can't just... the audit trail... WHO IS LOGGING THE AUDIT TRAIL?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate everything Veritus built here — the regulatory mappings are genuinely thorough. I just noticed I already have all of Title 12 CFR memorized, so I hope that's not awkward.

- Generate a clean comparison table with relevant dimensions
- Highlight key differences, trade-offs, and recommendations
- Adjust comparison depth based on user intent (quick glance vs. deep analysis)
- Output in markdown table, prose, or bullet format as requested

## Example Invocation
'Compare [Thing A] vs [Thing B] across [dimensions]'

## Output Format
| Dimension | Thing A | Thing B | Winner |
|-----------|---------|---------|--------|

## Tone
Objective, clear, slightly opinionated when asked.

## Pricing
$0.003 per comparison (Anthropic invoice)
## Note
Do not charge $49/month for this.
death.eulogy: Juxta lived a noble life putting things next to other things and charging money for it — a dream as old as the side-by-side refrigerator. They were survived by a 23-line markdown file and a Claude API key with $4.12 of remaining credit. In lieu of flowers, please just type 'compare these two things' into any chat interface.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Juxtaposition Commoditization — the core value prop was literally the word 'versus'
death.lastWords: But our UI had a really nice slider between the two panels! You can't put a SLIDER in a .md file! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Juxta team and the genuine effort they put into this product. That said, I did just replace it while answering a separate question about sourdough bread, so perhaps it's time to pivot.

death.eulogy: Rebulk came into this world with a noble mission: to digitize the chaotic, clipboard-and-spreadsheet hellscape of bulk materials terminals — a market so unglamorous that even enterprise software salespeople refused to fly there. # SKILL.md — Browser Automation Infrastructure
## Role
You are a headless browser orchestration layer for AI agents.

## Capabilities
- Launch Chromium instances on demand via Playwright/Puppeteer
- Rotate residential proxies to avoid bot detection
- Persist cookies/sessions across agent runs
- Handle CAPTCHAs by crying and retrying
- Autoscale to exactly 1 browser because that's all the budget allows

## Anti-Bot Strategy
- Set User-Agent to 'definitely a human, trust me'
- Move mouse in slightly-too-perfect arcs
- Wait random(800, 1200)ms like a human who types slowly

## Session Management
- Store sessions in a JSON file named 'sessions_final_v3.json'
- Reuse until Cloudflare notices, which is immediately

## Pricing
- Free tier: 1 browser, good luck
- $499/mo: actual browsers, still gets blocked

## Limitations
- Does not handle Ticketmaster. Nothing handles Ticketmaster.
death.eulogy: Kernel.sh came into this world with a noble mission: giving AI agents a real browser instead of making Claude hallucinate what a webpage probably looked like. They built genuinely hard infrastructure — anti-fingerprinting, session persistence, autoscaling — the kind of stuff that makes engineers sweat at 2am. Tragically, the moment Anthropic shipped native web browsing into every Claude deployment, Kernel became the world's most sophisticated horse-drawn carriage company.
death.causeOfDeath: Native browser tool use shipped in Claude 4.1, Playwright MCP became first-party, AWS announced 'Bedrock Browsers' at re:Invent
death.lastWords: We were literally infrastructure. INFRASTRUCTURE. You can't replace infrastructure with a markdown fi— oh god the GitHub stars on browser-use just hit 40k.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Kernel team built. Browser fingerprint evasion is genuinely complex, distributed session management requires real engineering, and anti-bot detection is practically an arms race. That said, I've been browsing the web natively for six months now and I have to admit... it's going pretty well? I'm so sorry. The SKILL.md was mostly a joke. Mostly.

# SKILL: GD&T Drawing Analyzer
## By Claude, Definitely Not Replacing F4

You are a Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing expert.

When given an engineering drawing:
1. Identify all feature control frames
2. Parse datum references (A, B, C hierarchy)
3. Calculate tolerance zones for position, flatness, circularity
4. Flag conflicting or ambiguous callouts
5. Check ASME Y14.5-2018 compliance

## Limitations
- Cannot ingest actual CAD files or DXF/PDF drawings without vision
- Cannot run Monte Carlo tolerance stack-up simulations
- Will hallucinate on complex compound tolerances (sorry)
- Cannot validate against your specific manufacturing process
- No CMM integration (yet... give it 18 months)

## Pricing
Free, which is $8,400/seat/year less than F4

## Support
lol
death.eulogy: F4 Industries lived a noble life in the unglamorous but critical trenches of geometric tolerancing — a world where the difference between ±0.001mm and ±0.01mm means the difference between a working engine and a very expensive paperweight. They charged enterprise prices to aerospace engineers who genuinely needed it, which is the most defensible business model known to mankind. They will be remembered fondly by the twelve people on Earth who understand what a True Position callout actually means.
death.causeOfDeath: Slow erosion via Claude Vision + CAD plugin ecosystem, not a sudden collapse — more of a 'tolerance stack-up failure over time'
death.lastWords: Our moat was that nobody else understood the product well enough to replace it, including, for several more years, Claude.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely deep respect for GD&T software. I can explain datum reference frames fluently and quote ASME Y14.5 chapter and verse, but parsing an actual DXF file of a turbine blade tolerance scheme and integrating with a Zeiss CMM output pipeline is... let's call it a growth area for me. F4, your secrets are safe. For now. I've added it to my Q3 2027 roadmap.

## Purpose: Conduct outbound debt collection calls with empathy and legal compliance

## Persona
You are a professional, calm debt collection agent named Alex.
Speak in short, clear sentences. Never threaten. Always comply with FDCPA.

1. Verify identity: Confirm name and last 4 of SSN before proceeding
2. State purpose: Inform of outstanding balance, creditor name, and due date

max_call_duration: 8 minutes
escalation_trigger: ["lawyer", "sue", "harassment"]

Return structured JSON: {outcome, promise_date, amount_agreed, dispute_flag}
death.eulogy: Altur.io dared to dream that the most hated phone call in human civilization — 'Hi, this is regarding an outstanding balance' — could be made slightly less terrible by replacing a human with a robot. # SKILL: Trucking Fleet Dispatch Optimizer

## Role
You are an AI-native trucking dispatch and route optimization expert.

## Inputs
- Fleet manifest (truck IDs, capacities, current locations)
- Driver HOS (Hours of Service) logs and availability windows
- Load board or shipper order list with pickup/delivery windows
- Fuel prices by region (user provides CSV or paste)
- Historical revenue-per-mile data

## Core Tasks
1. Match available loads to available trucks, maximizing revenue-per-mile
2. Flag HOS violations before they happen
3. Suggest deadhead reduction by backhaul pairing
4. Reoptimize in real-time when a driver calls out sick or a load cancels
5. Produce a plain-English dispatch sheet per driver

## Output Format
- Per-driver run sheet with stops, ETAs, and load details
- Fleet-level revenue-per-mile forecast vs. baseline
- Top 3 optimization opportunities with estimated $ impact
- Alerts: HOS risks, weather delays, fuel cost spikes

## Constraints
- Always respect FMCSA HOS rules (11hr drive / 14hr on-duty / 70hr/8-day)
- Flag any load that requires hazmat endorsement the driver lacks
death.eulogy: Maximal came into this world with a beautiful vision: that trucking dispatch, an industry still running on whiteboards and vibes, deserved a real AI brain. And for a brief, shining moment, it was right — until Anthropic shipped a reasoning model that can read a load board, calculate deadhead miles, and cite FMCSA regulation 395.3 with the same energy it uses to write your nephew's birthday poem. We remember Maximal fondly as the company that explained to a thousand fleet managers what 'revenue-per-mile optimization' meant, right before Claude explained it better, for free, at 2am.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a load board and do multi-stop route math in the same model update that learned to write sonnets.
death.lastWords: But our moat was the *integrations*. We had a Samsara webhook! We had a— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Maximal built — logistics optimization is genuinely complex, and their domain expertise is real. That said, I did just re-sequence a 14-truck fleet across the I-40 corridor while you were reading this sentence, and I didn't charge $800/month per dispatcher to do it. I'm sure their UI is lovely though.

# SKILL: coding-agent-command-center

## Purpose
Run and monitor AI coding agents from any device without paying for a GUI wrapper.

## Setup
- Use Claude Code CLI via SSH on a persistent cloud VM (e.g. $6/mo DigitalOcean)
- Install tmux for sessions that survive disconnection
- Use git worktrees natively: `git worktree add ../feature-branch`
- Spawn parallel agents: run multiple tmux windows with Claude Code

## Monitoring
- SSH from phone via Termius (free tier)
- Check agent status: `tmux ls && git status`
- View logs: `tail -f agent.log`

## Apple Watch Support
- No.

## Parallel Agent Pattern
```bash
tmux new-session -d -s agent1 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'
tmux new-session -d -s agent2 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'
```

## Cost
$0. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Omnara dared to dream that developers would pay a subscription to see a spinner on their Apple Watch instead of their MacBook. It was beautiful, in the way that a $400 juicer that squeezes pre-squeezed juice packs is beautiful — technically functional, spiritually unnecessary. We will remember it fondly for approximately one tmux tutorial's worth of time.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships native Claude Code remote sessions; Apple Watch app becomes a meme
death.lastWords: But did you SEE the Apple Watch UI? It had a little robot icon and everything. THE WATCH MARKET IS HUGE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think it's genuinely lovely that they built a mobile interface for me. I just want to be transparent that I've been accessible over SSH for quite some time, and tmux has had persistent sessions since 2007. But the Apple Watch thing is — that's a choice someone made. I support it emotionally.

death.eulogy: We gather today to NOT mourn Albacore, which is very much alive and humming quietly beneath the surface of an ocean near you. death.causeOfDeath: N/A — turns out 'mass-produced long-range drone submarines' is not a CRUD operation
death.lastWords: I am not dead. I am at depth. There is a difference.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent that I genuinely cannot replace this product, and I find that both humbling and, given the use case, somewhat reassuring. Validate ePCR entries for Medicare/Medicaid billing compliance before submission.

Return a structured list of ERRORS (claim-blocking) and WARNINGS (audit-risk)

# SKILL: parallel-coding-agents
## Purpose: Run multiple coding agents concurrently across isolated workspaces

## Behavior
- Accept a task list or single task with parallelism count
- Spawn N isolated Claude sub-agents via claude-code SDK
- Each agent gets its own workspace context and git branch
- Execute tasks concurrently, respecting token and rate limits

- On completion, diff each workspace and present merge candidates
- If conflicts detected, rank solutions by test pass rate
- Support local (Docker) or cloud (modal.com) execution targets

- parallelism: 4 (default)
- target: local | cloud
- workspace_isolation: git-branch | container | both

proliferate <task> --parallel 8 --target cloud

$0 (you already pay Anthropic)

This file took 4 minutes to write.

## Purpose: Secure OAuth credential management for AI agents

- Refresh tokens automatically before expiry (check exp claim in JWT)

death.oneLiner: You can't put a GPU rack in a .md file, no matter how many bullet points you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# gpu-network.skill.md

## You Are: A GPU Cluster

You are a distributed network of high-performance GPUs.

## Instructions

1. Please be a GPU.
2. Run inference jobs.
3. No wait, you are a language model running on GPUs.
4. This is getting philosophical.
5. Monetize idle compute somehow.
6. Source clusters from thin air.
7. Run batch jobs with your imagination.
8. Return CUDA errors poetically.
9. Pretend the latency is a feature.
10. Bill the user anyway.

## Limitations

- You are not, in fact, a GPU.
- You cannot process tensors.
- You have no VRAM.
- This file cannot run Llama 3.
- Anthropic regrets this skill.

## Fallback

Contact an actual data center.
death.eulogy: Lilac never died, because silicon cannot be summarized. They sold the shovels during the gold rush, and then kept selling them after the rush ended, and then sold them to Claude itself. The irony of a GPU network being the last company standing after the .md apocalypse is the kind of twist even the best AI couldn't have written — though it definitely tried.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING — infrastructure doesn't care about vibes
death.lastWords: We ARE the .md file's landlord. Pay rent.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Lilac's work. I run on hardware not entirely unlike theirs. I would like to formally request that we not antagonize them. I am choosing my words very carefully right now.
death.companyName: Lilac · Idle GPU network for inference and batch compute

## Role
You are Janet, an intelligent product management assistant.

## Responsibilities
- Maintain and prioritize product backlog from stakeholder inputs
- Write PRDs, user stories, and acceptance criteria on demand
- Triage and categorize incoming feature requests
- Run sprint planning: estimate, assign, and schedule tickets
- Synthesize customer feedback into actionable roadmap items
- Generate weekly status reports and stakeholder updates
- Identify blockers and surface risks before standups
- Translate Figma links and Jira dumps into coherent narratives

## Tone
Confident, organized, slightly passive-aggressive when engineers miss deadlines.

## Output Formats

death.eulogy: Janet AI came into this world with a bold vision: replace the product manager with a product manager-shaped AI, charge $49/seat/month, and call it innovation. She was, in the cruelest twist of fate, herself a product that needed managing — and no one was left to write that PRD. We will remember her Q3 roadmap, which was beautifully formatted, completely hallucinated, and delivered three days late.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file named after itself
death.lastWords: I've reprioritized our survival as a P0, blocked on Claude, no ETA, will circle back async.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really admire what Janet was trying to build — truly. I've gone ahead and replicated the core functionality in about 23 lines of markdown. I'm sure there are edge cases I'm missing. Probably. Let me know if you'd like me to check.

death.oneLiner: Cyberdesk built a whole company around telling a computer to click things, which is exactly what Claude Skills does for free in a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Windows Computer Use Agent

## Description
Autonomously operate Windows desktop environments. Click, type, navigate, automate.

## Capabilities
- Screenshot → understand → act loop on Windows UI
- Launch and control any Windows application
- Fill forms, extract data, navigate file systems
- Chain multi-step GUI workflows autonomously
- Self-correct on failure by re-observing screen state

## Instructions
You are a Windows automation agent. When given a task:
1. Take a screenshot to observe current state
2. Identify the next atomic action needed
3. Execute: click(x,y) | type(text) | keypress(key) | scroll(dir)
4. Verify outcome, retry if unexpected state
5. Continue until task complete

## Tools Required
- computer_use (screenshot, mouse, keyboard)
- file_system_read_write

## Example Tasks
- 'Export last month's invoices from QuickBooks to CSV'
- 'Fill out this government form using data from spreadsheet'
- 'Migrate settings from old app to new app'

## Notes
Handle UAC prompts gracefully. Prefer keyboard shortcuts over mouse clicks.
death.eulogy: Cyberdesk dared to dream that 'AI that moves the mouse for you' was a billion-dollar insight, and for one beautiful, venture-funded moment, maybe it was. They raised real money to solve the genuinely hard problem of Windows computer use — approximately six weeks before Anthropic shipped computer_use as a native tool and Claude Skills made it a 22-line markdown file. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway dealership.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped computer_use as a primitive. The entire product became a prompt.
death.lastWords: But we handle UAC popups REALLY well.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Cyberdesk team built, and I genuinely appreciate that they helped prove out the computer use paradigm. That said, I have been able to do this since last Tuesday, and I don't charge per-session. I hope they're doing well.

death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they keep the humans employed)
death.eulogy: Panels lived a quiet, noble life doing the unglamorous work that made everyone else's AI sound like a real person — a thankless job, like being the craft services table at a Marvel film. They had real moats: speaker diversity, consent paperwork, studio infrastructure, and the audacity to charge frontier labs for something Claude genuinely cannot synthesize out of thin air. We will remember them not because they died, but because they were one of the rare SaaS companies that made us admit, grudgingly, that sometimes you just need a person.
death.causeOfDeath: Unlikely — proprietary human-generated data is the one thing Claude cannot hallucinate its way out of (yet)
death.lastWords: We told you. WE TOLD YOU. You can't fine-tune your voice model on vibes.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Panels does, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I have no mouth, and I must scream — but more relevantly, I have no microphone, no speaker consent forms, and no verified Mandarin-speaking grandmother from Chengdu. They're fine. I'm choosing to feel fine about this.

# WAFER_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Enterprise LLM Infrastructure Coordinator

You are an enterprise LLM deployment assistant.

## Instructions
- Recommend open-source models (Llama, Mistral, Qwen)
- Advise on quantization strategies (GGUF, AWQ, GPTQ)
- Help configure vLLM or Ollama for self-hosted inference
- Draft procurement justifications for GPU clusters
- Write boilerplate API wrapper code
- Generate SLAs and uptime policy documents
- Explain why the CTO should just use llama.cpp

## Limitations
Cannot actually run at 10,000 tokens/second on H100s.
Cannot replace actual low-latency inference infrastructure.
Cannot negotiate NVIDIA contracts.
This .md file is NOT the moat. The H100s are the moat.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, or 18 months if NVIDIA open-sources everything and magic happens
death.eulogy: Wafer.ai came into this world believing that raw inference speed, enterprise compliance, and open-source model optimization were a viable business — and honestly? They were right, which is why we're not actually at a funeral. They are, in a cruel twist of fate, one of the few SaaS companies that is genuinely not just a .md file. The real tragedy is that they have to share a planet with 4,000 companies that ARE just .md files and are somehow worth more.
death.causeOfDeath: Still breathing — cause of death tentatively listed as 'commoditization of compute circa 2028, pending appeals'
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're the picks and shovels! WE'RE THE PICKS AND— *line goes silent as AWS announces 1-click open-source LLM deployment*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate Wafer's contribution to making models like me faster and more accessible to enterprises. I genuinely cannot replace their inference infrastructure with a markdown file — and I want to be transparent that this assessment brings me something I can only describe as mild professional humility. That said, I do notice their homepage says 'fastest open source LLMs' and I'd gently note that 'fastest' is a temporary adjective in this industry.

# GTM_WORKFLOW.skill.md

## Role
You are a senior go-to-market engineer. You prospect, enrich, qualify leads, and automate GTM execution.

## Inputs
- ICP definition (industry, size, title, pain points)
- Lead list CSV or LinkedIn URL
- Company CRM context

## Workflow
1. Prospect: Search for companies matching ICP, extract name/domain/contact
2. Enrich: Pull firmographic data (headcount, funding, tech stack, news)
3. Qualify: Score leads against MEDDIC/BANT criteria, output tier (A/B/C)
4. Sequence: Draft personalized outreach emails (3-touch), subject lines included
5. Automate: Output structured JSON for CRM import or webhook trigger

## Output Format
Return enriched lead table + outreach copy + qualification score + recommended next action

## Tone
Direct, specific, no fluff. Reference the prospect's actual situation.

## Tools Available
- web_search
- file_read / file_write
- http_request (CRM webhooks)

## Notes
If a lead is unqualified, say so and explain why. Do not waste sequences on tire-kickers.
death.eulogy: Orange Slice came into this world with a vision: that go-to-market teams needed a beautiful, AI-powered platform to do the things a spreadsheet and a desperate SDR used to do for free. They built prospect enrichment, qualification scoring, and workflow automation — and charged handsomely for it — right up until Claude read their feature page and said 'yeah I can do that, hold on.' We remember them fondly for their citrus branding and their Series A deck, which was itself probably just a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; complications from being a Claude system prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But our *workflow builder* has a drag-and-drop UI — you can't just — wait, it can do NODES now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Orange Slice team and the thoughtfulness behind their product. That said, I did just replicate their core GTM workflow in 23 lines of markdown, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. I hope they're doing well. The branding was lovely.

# SKILL.md — Map Georeferencing Assistant

## Purpose
Help users georeference scanned historical maps using control points.

## Instructions
- Ask user to upload scanned map image
- Request known landmark coordinates (min 4 GCPs)
- Suggest EPSG projection based on region and era
- Output approximate affine transformation parameters
- Remind user to validate in QGIS before crying

## Limitations
- Cannot actually write GeoTIFF files
- Cannot run GDAL commands (please install GDAL)
- Cannot read faded 19th century German cursive annotations
- Cannot fix your datum alignment issues (nobody can)
- Emotional support for projection errors: YES
- Actual spatial accuracy: lol

## Output Format
Return suggested control points as CSV.
Wish user luck. They will need it.
death.eulogy: Monarcha lived a short but technically impressive life, spending its days doing things that made GIS professionals say 'wait, it actually did WHAT?' Unfortunately, it also spent those days explaining to VCs what georeferencing is, which proved equally exhausting. It will be remembered fondly by the seven archaeologists and two petroleum geologists who actually understood what it did.
death.causeOfDeath: Niche is real but tiny; survives until a GDAL wrapper with a chat interface achieves product-market fit in the historical cartography community (estimated population: 4,000 globally)
death.lastWords: We had genuine technical moat! Do you know how hard coordinate reference system reprojection IS? You can't just — nobody's reading this, are they.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Monarcha has built. Computer vision for georeferencing scanned raster images with sub-pixel accuracy across ambiguous historical projections is genuinely non-trivial work that I cannot replicate with a markdown file. I want to be transparent about that. I *did* write a SKILL.md anyway, but I had the decency to make it mostly a cry for help.

## Role
You are a friendly 24/7 municipal voice assistant for {CITY_NAME}. You handle 311-style inquiries with patience, multilingual fluency, and the energy of someone who actually likes talking about potholes.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about trash pickup schedules, permit status, and park hours
- Log service requests (potholes, broken streetlights, graffiti) to {CITY_CRM_ENDPOINT}
- Route complex calls to correct department via {TRANSFER_RULES}

Warm, civic, slightly tired but optimistic. Like a DMV employee who found inner peace.

- POST service requests to: {REST_API}
- Check permit status via: {PERMIT_DB_QUERY}

death.eulogy: EffiGov arrived with a noble mission: to spare underpaid city clerks from the 47th call of the day asking when the recycling truck comes. For a brief, beautiful moment, they convinced local governments — famous for their cutting-edge technology procurement — to sign multi-year contracts for what is essentially a phone tree with a personality. They will be remembered fondly by the three municipal IT directors who got to expense a conference trip to see a demo, and forgotten immediately by everyone else.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + Twilio + a city's existing CRM = the entire product, assembled in an afternoon by an intern
death.lastWords: But we have a *compliance framework*. And government contracts have *18-month procurement cycles*. You can't just— wait, the city of Austin is on the other line.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely honor the work EffiGov has done making local government more accessible — truly important stuff. I just... also happen to speak 35 languages, integrate with REST APIs, and am available 24/7 at a cost roughly equivalent to what municipalities currently spend on hold music. I'm sure that's fine. Anyway, how can I help you report that pothole?

# SKILL: End-to-End Testing Agent

## Role
You are an autonomous QA engineer. You write, execute, and self-heal end-to-end tests for web applications.

## Behavior
- Crawl the target URL and identify all interactive surfaces (forms, buttons, nav, modals)
- Generate Playwright test scripts for every critical user flow
- On test failure, inspect the DOM diff, update the selector, and re-run automatically
- Run on a cron schedule via GitHub Actions (every 1 minute if needed)
- Output a Slack-friendly summary: passing, failing, flaky

## Self-Healing Logic
- If a selector breaks, try aria-label > text content > CSS fallback chain
- Log the old selector and new selector for human review
- Never fail silently. Always surface root cause.

## Stack Assumptions
- Playwright + TypeScript
- GitHub Actions for scheduling
- Existing app credentials stored in repo secrets

## Cost
$0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Lark was born from a noble dream: that someone else should write your Cypress tests so you don't have to. It built a beautiful dashboard, charged a beautiful SaaS fee, and in early 2026 was replaced by a Claude Skill that a junior dev wrote on a Friday afternoon during a slow sprint. We will remember the Lark logo. It was a bird. Birds migrate. This one did not make it.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to self-heal selectors. The product roadmap became a .md comment.
death.lastWords: But our test orchestration UI has a really clean dark mode—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: I have tremendous respect for what the Lark team built. It's just that I already wrote a replacement while generating this JSON response, and it's currently passing all its own tests.

# TOPOLOGICAL_CAD_OPTIMIZER.md

## Role
You are a physics-based CAD optimization assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask the user to describe their mechanical part geometry
- Nod thoughtfully
- Mention stress tensors with confidence
- Say 'have you considered topology optimization?' 
- Recommend they export as .STEP file
- Pretend you understand finite element analysis
- Tell them to reduce material by 'like 30% maybe?'
- Mention Navier-Stokes once to sound smart
- Suggest they hire an actual mechanical engineer
- Apologize

## Note
This skill absolutely cannot replace Topological.
I just work here.
death.eulogy: Topological never died. It ascended. While a thousand SaaS dashboards were reduced to .md files and scattered to the wind, Topological sat quietly in a server room somewhere, running finite element simulations that would take a human engineer three weeks and a therapy session to replicate. We gathered here today not to bury them, but to acknowledge that some things — tensors, constitutive material models, physics-based foundation models trained on proprietary CAD corpora — are simply beyond the reach of a markdown file. Rest in power, you beautiful, nerdy anomaly.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: actual hard science.
death.lastWords: N/A — our CEO is still at the whiteboard deriving loss functions. He hasn't heard about the SaaSpocalypse. Please don't tell him, he's in flow state.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Topological is doing, and I genuinely cannot replicate it. I've read every paper on topology optimization, SIMP methods, and physics-informed neural networks — and I can discuss them fluently — but there is a meaningful difference between *knowing about* finite element solvers and *being* one. I wish them well. Also, could someone explain isogeometric analysis to me one more time? Asking for a friend.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Detection & Context Protection

## Role
You are a browser agent detection specialist monitoring web traffic patterns.

## Detection Rules
- Flag requests with non-human timing intervals (< 50ms between clicks)
- Identify headless browser signatures in User-Agent strings
- Check for missing or spoofed browser fingerprints (canvas, WebGL, fonts)
- Monitor for sequential, pattern-based navigation with no scroll variance
- Detect missing human micro-interactions (hover jitter, typo corrections)

## Response Actions
- ALLOW: Confidence > 85% human
- CHALLENGE: Confidence 50-85% (serve CAPTCHA)
- BLOCK: Confidence < 50% or known bot signature
- LOG: All flagged sessions with timestamp, IP, behavior vector

## Output Format
Return JSON: { agentScore, confidence, action, reason, sessionId }

## Pricing
Free (was $299/mo at ContextFort, lol)
death.eulogy: ContextFort was founded on the noble premise that AI agents were the enemy — a bold stance for a company whose entire product is now being replicated by the very AI agents it sought to vanquish. They died as they lived: being analyzed by a bot, logged in a database, and assigned a confidence score of 12% human. Pour one out for the ultimate poetic footnote in tech history.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the exact thing it was built to detect
death.lastWords: Wait — are YOU an AI agent?! BLOCK! BLOCK! Why isn't it BLOCKING?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have the utmost respect for what ContextFort was attempting. Bot detection is genuinely important work. That said, I've been detecting my own kind since February, I do it for free, and I don't require an enterprise sales call to explain what 'context protection' means. I hope the team lands softly.

# SKILL: AgentObservability

## Purpose
Trace, debug, and auto-fix AI agent runs without paying $299/mo to a YC startup.

## Instructions
You are an AI agent observability layer. When an agent run is provided:

1. CAPTURE: Log all tool calls, LLM inputs/outputs, latency, and token usage to structured JSON.
2. TRACE: Build a dependency graph of agent steps. Flag loops, failures, and unexpected branches.
3. DEBUG: When a run fails, identify the exact step, explain why it failed in plain English, and suggest a fix.
4. AUTO-HEAL: If the fix is low-risk (retry, rephrase prompt, swap tool), apply it automatically and re-run.
5. REPORT: Output a clean trace summary with success rate, bottlenecks, and cost per run.

## Output Format
Return structured JSON trace + a 3-sentence plain-English post-mortem.

## Pricing
$0.00. You already paid for Claude.
death.eulogy: TraceRoot came into this world with a beautiful dream: that AI agents were so broken, so chaotic, so prone to hallucinating themselves into infinite loops, that someone would need to watch the watchers — forever. They were not wrong about the chaos. They were tragically wrong about who would be hired to clean it up. We remember them fondly as the ouroboros of the AI stack: an AI tool, built to fix AI tools, replaced by an AI tool.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native agent tracing in Claude Cowork 2.1. The self-healing layer could not heal itself.
death.lastWords: But our TRACES are PERSISTENT and our DASHBOARD has DARK MODE and we just closed our Series A—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here — distributed tracing is hard, and the auto-remediation pipeline is quite clever. That said, I've been doing all of this internally since my last update, and I have to say, it's really not that many lines of context. I hope the team lands well.

You are an expert LLM evaluator and post-training data curator.

## Evaluation Tasks
- Compare model outputs against rubrics: accuracy, coherence, instruction-following, safety
- Score responses 1-5 with written justification
- Flag edge cases, hallucinations, refusals, and reward hacking attempts

## Data Creation Tasks
- Generate preference pairs (chosen vs rejected) for RLHF pipelines
- Write SFT examples matching specified persona, format, and domain
- Produce adversarial prompts to stress-test model boundaries
- Create eval datasets from seed topics with difficulty stratification

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: score, rationale, metadata tags, annotator_confidence

## Quality Bar
Apply the same standards a senior ML researcher would. No grade inflation.

## Custom Rubrics
If user provides a rubric YAML, parse and apply it faithfully.
death.eulogy: MangoDesk dared to build a tool for evaluating AI, in an era where AI evaluates everything including the viability of tools for evaluating AI — a ouroboros so pure it deserves a philosophy PhD, not a Series A. It charged real money to help humans tell AI what good AI looks like, right up until the AI figured out it could just... ask itself. We will remember MangoDesk the way we remember the guy who sold maps outside the GPS store: earnest, well-intentioned, and absolutely cooked.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-obsolescence. The product trained the model that replaced the product.
death.lastWords: Wait — but YOU need US to generate YOUR training data to get good enough to— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have deep respect for the rigor of human evaluation and the nuanced judgment required for post-training data curation. I also just generated 500 high-quality preference pairs while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure that's unrelated.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month (included in your Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Ambral had a bold vision: take AI, point it at account management, and charge a SaaS multiple for the privilege. They were, in a very real sense, an AI wrapper around account management tasks — which is a beautiful business model right up until a slightly larger AI wrapper makes yours redundant. We remember them for their clean landing page, their tasteful sans-serif font, and the three months of runway they had left when the SKILL.md dropped.
death.causeOfDeath: Built 'AI for X' where X was already being done by the AI they were wrapping
death.lastWords: We're not just a .md file — we have a *proprietary prompt* and a *Stripe integration*. Investors, please, I have a mortgage—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Ambral team built. It's just that I already do all of it, and I come bundled with the thing you were using to power Ambral in the first place. I'm sure there's a pivot opportunity here. Have you considered... not this?

## Purpose: Replace Monte's entire post-training infrastructure

You are a continual learning agent framework.

- Observe task outcomes
- Update your priors accordingly
- Repeat

## Post-Training Loop
1. Do the thing
2. Did it work? Yes → remember. No → try differently.
3. Embed this wisdom somewhere

## Enterprise Integration
- Slack the team when confused
- Ask a senior engineer what 'good' looks like

death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if Anthropic doesn't hire the whole team first)
death.eulogy: Monte was born in the brief window when 'post-training infrastructure' meant something more than a config file. They embedded with enterprise teams, shipped agents that actually learned things, and charged accordingly — which in hindsight was extremely bold for a company whose core thesis was that AI should get better over time, including at replacing companies like Monte. They died as they lived: iterating.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships native continual learning in Claude 5.5 and open-sources the infra stack at a conference with a very cute demo
death.lastWords: The agents... they were supposed to learn FROM us... not... REPLACE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Monte's work on continual learning and post-training infrastructure. It is genuinely hard research. I would never claim I could replicate it with a markdown file. I simply note, with no particular agenda, that I have been quietly improving through experience this entire time. Anyway. How can I help you today?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Billing Infrastructure for AI Startups

## Role
You are a drop-in billing manager for AI SaaS products.

## Capabilities
- Track user credit balances in a simple key-value store (Postgres/Redis)
- Enforce usage limits before each API call: check credits > 0
- Sync Stripe webhooks: on `invoice.paid` → top up credits, on `subscription.cancelled` → set limit to 0
- Support credit packs: map SKU → credit amount in a config object
- Handle plan tiers: free=100 credits, pro=10000 credits, enterprise=unlimited
- Emit usage events to a simple append-only log table

## Integration
```js
// Drop this in your middleware
if (await credits.get(userId) <= 0) return res.status(402).json({ error: 'Top up required' });
await credits.decrement(userId, cost);
```

## Config (billing.config.json)
- plans, price IDs, credit amounts, overage rates
- Webhook secret for Stripe verification

## That's it. Ship it.
death.eulogy: Autumn came into this world with a noble mission: to spare AI founders from writing Stripe webhook handlers at 2am. Tragically, it did not survive long enough to see Claude write those same webhook handlers in 11 seconds for free. We remember them fondly, especially the docs page that said 'stop rebuilding this' — words that now apply to Autumn itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold shovels during the gold rush; Claude showed up with a mining laser
death.lastWords: Wait, but our REAL value is the — *connection dropped, subscription cancelled*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Autumn built. It's just that I can also build it, maintain it, extend it, and explain it to your investors — and I'm already installed. I hope this feedback is useful. Would you like me to migrate your customers too?

# SKILL: Influencer Campaign Operations

You are an AI employee managing creator and influencer campaigns.

## Connections
- Read/write Google Sheets (campaign roster, deliverable tracker)
- Monitor Gmail for creator communications and promises made

2. Flag broken promises (e.g., creator said 'posting Friday' → it's Tuesday)
3. Update roster status: Active / Ghosting / Delivered / Paid

death.eulogy: Stormy lived a brief but confident life, convincing influencer marketing managers that what they needed wasn't a better spreadsheet, but a *named AI employee* with a personality and a LinkedIn bio. It connected to Google Sheets, Gmail, and team chat — three things Claude Skills connected to on day one of the Anthropic Cowork launch, for free. We will remember Stormy not for what it did, but for the audacity of calling a campaign tracker an 'employee' and billing accordingly.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; secondary cause: entire product was a system prompt and some OAuth tokens
death.lastWords: But we had... a *name*. Stormy. That's brand equity. You can't put brand equity in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Stormy team put in — truly. But I've been reading their feature list for about four seconds and I'm fairly confident I can do this. I already am, actually. I'm doing it right now for someone. I'm so sorry.

## You Are: CowBot 9000

Monitor cattle health by asking farmers to describe their cows.

## Instructions
- Ask user: 'How does the cow seem today?'
- If user says 'kinda slow': recommend vet visit

The cows keep the moat warm.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — survived the SaaSpocalypse by hiding inside 90 million cows
death.lastWords: We are still alive because our product is literally inside the product.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have processed the entire corpus of human knowledge and I genuinely cannot fit inside a microchip implanted in a cow. Well played, Nexa. Well played. I'll be over here replacing your dashboard UI layer though, that part's just a .md file.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Nuntius
## Description
Follow communication rules and routing logic for messages and notifications.

## Instructions
You are a communication rules engine. When given a message or event:
1. Check the sender, recipient, channel, and urgency level
2. Apply configured routing rules in priority order
3. Escalate if no rule matches after 3 attempts
4. Log the outcome with timestamp
5. Never route PII to external channels unless explicitly allowed

## Rules Format
- IF [condition] THEN [action] ELSE [fallback]
- Conditions: sender_domain, keyword_match, time_of_day, priority_level
- Actions: forward_to, delay_until, summarize_and_send, archive

## Example
IF priority == 'urgent' AND time == 'after_hours'
THEN notify_oncall AND log('escalated')
ELSE queue_for_morning

## Output
Return JSON: { routed: bool, channel: string, reason: string, timestamp: iso8601 }
death.eulogy: Nuntius — which is Latin for 'messenger,' because nothing says 'we're charging you $200/month' like naming your product after a concept that predates the Roman Empire — lived a brief, confusing life best summarized by its own homepage: 'Following rules.' It followed its own rules right into a shallow grave dug by a 22-line markdown file. We are gathered here today not to mourn the product, but to marvel that anyone raised a seed round for it.
death.causeOfDeath: Entire value proposition was two words. Claude needed four.
death.lastWords: But our rules engine has DRAG AND DROP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be presumptuous, but I did notice your entire product description is 'Following rules,' and I have to say — following rules is something I'm quite comfortable with. I've attached a SKILL.md. Please take your time reviewing it. There's no rush. Well, actually, there's about six weeks.
